title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore the history and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will learn about its traditional role as a source of kai (eels, watercress), its spiritual significance to Ngāti Kahungunu, and contemporary challenges facing its health and mauri.
learningObjectives: Investigate and explain the historical and cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to Ngāti Kahungunu, using at least three specific examples.
Analyze human impact on the awa by collecting and interpreting water quality data (e.g., clarity, pH, macroinvertebrate count) from two different sites.
Collaboratively design and present a practical kaitiakitanga action plan to enhance the health of the awa, incorporating both mātauranga Māori and scientific perspectives.

activities.title: Whakapapa o te Awa: Establishing Our Connection
activities.description: Students begin by locating Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku on maps (both topographical and Māori land maps). A local kaumātua or representative from a local marae visits the class to share the pūrākau (story) of the awa, its original name, and its importance as a source of sustenance and identity. Students create an illustrated timeline of the awa's history.

activities.title: Tiaki Taiao: Field Investigation
activities.description: Guided by a local environmental ranger, students conduct a field trip to two sites along the awa: one upstream near a more natural source and one downstream closer to urban influence. Using water testing kits, they collect data on clarity, pH, and temperature. They also use kick nets to sample macroinvertebrates, which serve as bio-indicators of stream health.

activities.title: He Kōrero, He Kōrero: Analysing Perspectives
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students analyse their field data to assess the health of the awa. They then research and compare different perspectives on its management. This includes reading a local council resource management plan, articles from the Hawke's Bay Today, and statements from local iwi environmental groups like Manaaki Te Awa.

activities.title: Manaaki i te Awa: Designing an Action Plan
activities.description: In small groups, students synthesize their learning to create a detailed kaitiakitanga action plan. The plan must propose one tangible action (e.g., organizing a community planting day for natives, designing educational signage, creating a public awareness campaign) and justify it using evidence from their field investigation and cultural research.

activities.title: Whakamana i te Kaupapa: Sharing Our Learning
activities.description: Groups present their action plans to the class and an invited audience, which could include the kaumātua, the environmental ranger, and a representative from the college's board. The best ideas are compiled and formally submitted to the local kaitiaki group or council for consideration.

resources: Kaumātua or representative from a local Ngāti Kahungunu marae (e.g., Omahu Marae)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Wai Ora' water quality monitoring kits and identification guides
NZ Topographic Map (LINZ) and Papakāinga Maps showing original Māori place names
Manaaki Te Awa Trust website and resources on local freshwater restoration projects

tasks: An individual research report on the historical and cultural significance of the awa.
A group field investigation portfolio containing data, analysis, and conclusions.
A group presentation of the proposed kaitiakitanga action plan.

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges.
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies.
Social Studies L4: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability.
Science: Living World L4: Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes, both natural and human-induced.
